case_name: 1,2-Dibromoethane removal from surface water for a small drinking water system
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for removing 1,2-dibromoethane from surface water intended for drinking water supply under low-energy, small-system constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small town draws drinking water from a river-influenced surface water source near agricultural land with historical fumigant use. Monitoring has identified 1,2-dibromoethane as the primary regulated organic contaminant of concern, while general surface water treatment is already needed for turbidity and pathogen control. The utility prefers established technologies that can be operated by a small staff with low energy demand and manageable residuals.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should comply with drinking water requirements for 1,2-dibromoethane and provide a conventional microbial barrier suitable for potable supply.

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: limited capacity for hazardous liquid residuals; periodic spent media replacement is acceptable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: reliable year-round operation with routine operator attention only

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: reduce suspended solids and lower particulate and natural organic matter loading ahead of adsorption
core_unit_functions.sedimentation_or_clarification: remove formed flocs to protect downstream filters and carbon beds
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish remaining turbidity and provide conventional surface water particle removal
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb 1,2-dibromoethane and provide practical VOC control in a low-energy fixed-bed process
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbial inactivation and distribution system protection

applicability_rationale: 1,2-dibromoethane is a halogenated organic contaminant that is well addressed by activated carbon adsorption in established drinking water practice
the source is surface water, so conventional solids removal is appropriate ahead of adsorption to reduce fouling and extend carbon life
the process train is compatible with a small utility that needs low-energy operation and proven unit processes
granular activated carbon provides targeted removal without the complexity and energy burden of high-pressure membranes
potential_risks: breakthrough can occur if carbon replacement or regeneration intervals are not monitored carefully
higher natural organic matter or seasonal turbidity spikes can shorten bed life
pretreatment underperformance may increase headloss and reduce adsorption efficiency

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: reduce upstream solids and organic loading from surface water
core_unit_functions.filtration: provide low-turbidity water to downstream air-stripping equipment
core_unit_functions.packed_tower_aeration: transfer volatile 1,2-dibromoethane from water to air for removal
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: 1,2-dibromoethane is a volatile organic compound, so air stripping is technically effective under suitable design conditions
this is a recognized treatment approach for volatile organics in drinking water
it can achieve strong contaminant removal without high-pressure operation
it ranks below GAC because off-gas management and aeration equipment are less attractive for a small low-energy system
potential_risks: off-gas treatment or air emissions review may be required for stripped volatile contaminants
tower fouling or scaling can increase maintenance burden
cold weather and variable flow can affect stripping performance

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: promote particle aggregation and reduce raw water variability
core_unit_functions.sedimentation_or_clarification: remove bulk solids and formed floc
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: adsorb a portion of dissolved 1,2-dibromoethane when dosed into the treatment train
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: capture carbon fines and remaining solids

applicability_rationale: activated carbon is aligned with the contaminant type, and powdered carbon may be considered where fixed GAC contactors are not immediately available
the train remains based on conventional surface water treatment steps familiar to small utilities
it can serve as an interim or lower-capital option for organics control
it ranks below GAC because PAC typically offers less stable long-term control for a specific dissolved contaminant and requires reliable chemical handling
potential_risks: removal efficiency can be variable and contact time is less controlled than in fixed-bed GAC
ongoing carbon dosing increases solids production and sludge handling needs
operator optimization is needed to respond to seasonal raw water changes

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: prepare surface water by removing particulate matter and some natural organic matter
core_unit_functions.filtration: provide clarified water for downstream oxidation and polishing
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: chemically degrade target organic contaminants using strong oxidant and radical chemistry

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can be technically effective for recalcitrant organic contaminants in some treatment contexts
GAC polishing can support removal of oxidation byproducts and residual trace organics
the approach is more complex than needed for a straightforward small-system 1,2-dibromoethane case
it ranks lower because the scenario explicitly favors low energy and simple operation
potential_risks: formation of oxidation byproducts requires control and monitoring
process performance depends strongly on oxidant dose, UV or peroxide management, and organic matrix effects
higher technical skill and instrumentation are needed than preferred for the utility

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: reduce particulate and colloidal loading before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.filtration: protect membrane elements from suspended matter
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis_or_nanofiltration: provide broad contaminant rejection through pressure-driven membrane separation
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after desalting treatment
core_unit_functions.disinfection: maintain microbiological safety in finished water

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment can reduce a wide range of contaminants and is technically capable as a barrier process
it is not the preferred first choice for a single VOC problem in a small system with low-energy constraints
the process introduces unnecessary pressure, concentrate, and post-treatment complexity compared with adsorption or air stripping
it should rank last because simpler established technologies are more proportionate to the treatment objective
potential_risks: high specific energy demand and membrane replacement cost
concentrate management is difficult for small systems
pretreatment and fouling control add operational burden beyond what is needed for this case

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based treatment with conventional surface water pretreatment should rank first
air_stripping may rank high as an established alternative for volatile contaminants but generally below the simplest small-system low-energy option
high-pressure membrane options should not rank first under low-energy small-system constraints

must_reflect: small-system preference for simple and proven treatment trains
need for conventional surface water particle removal and disinfection in addition to contaminant control

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for 1,2-dibromoethane control
why air stripping is relevant because the contaminant is volatile
why conventional pretreatment is needed for surface water ahead of specialized removal
why reverse_osmosis_or_nanofiltration ranks lower under the stated constraints

should_mention: carbon breakthrough and media replacement monitoring
off-gas handling or emissions considerations for air stripping
raw water organic matter and turbidity impacts on treatment performance
